description: Vulnerabilities specific to multi-tenant SaaS architectures where multiple customers share infrastructure. Focuses on DynamoDB shared-table patterns, S3 prefix-based isolation, and Cognito JWT-based tenant context.

entries.name: IDOR via Tenant ID in Request Body
entries.description: User changes tenant_id/customer_id in request body to access another tenant's data.
entries.how_it_works: Application uses tenant identifier from request body (user-controlled) rather than the authenticated JWT context to construct database keys. Attacker modifies the ID in their request to query another tenant's partition.
preconditions: Tenant ID extracted from request body (not auth context)
DynamoDB partition key constructed with this ID
No server-side validation of body ID against auth ID
entries.detection_guidance: Trace backward from DynamoDB Key construction: does the tenant_id variable originate from event['body'] or from event['requestContext']['authorizer']?
entries.vulnerable_code:
body = json.loads(event['body'])
customer_id = body.get('customer_id')
response = table.query(
    KeyConditionExpression=Key('pk').eq(f'TENANT#{customer_id}')
)
remediation: Always use tenant_id from JWT/auth context, never from request body
Add middleware that automatically injects authenticated tenant_id
IAM condition on DynamoDB LeadingKeys as defense-in-depth

entries.name: S3 Key Path Traversal Across Tenants
entries.description: User-controlled filename in S3 key allows reading/writing objects in another tenant's prefix.
entries.how_it_works: Application constructs S3 keys as `{tenant_id}/{filename}`. If filename isn't sanitized, attacker provides `../../other-tenant/secret.pdf` to escape their prefix.
preconditions: S3 key includes user-controlled filename
No path normalization (os.path.basename)
No post-construction prefix validation
impact: Plant forged evidence in another tenant's space
entries.detection_guidance: Find S3 key construction with user-supplied filename. Check for os.path.basename() or equivalent sanitization.
entries.vulnerable_code:
filename = body['filename']
key = f'{tenant_id}/{filename}'
s3.put_object(Bucket=BUCKET, Key=key, Body=content)
entries.safe_code:
filename = os.path.basename(body['filename'])[:255]
assert filename and '..' not in filename
upload_id = str(uuid4())
key = f'{auth_tenant_id}/evidence/{upload_id}/{filename}'
assert key.startswith(f'{auth_tenant_id}/')
s3.put_object(Bucket=BUCKET, Key=key, Body=content)
remediation: Strip path with os.path.basename()
Add UUID to prevent predictable keys
Validate final key starts with expected tenant prefix
Use tenant_id from auth context

entries.name: DynamoDB Shared Table Without IAM LeadingKey Condition
entries.description: Lambda IAM role grants full table access without restricting to specific partition key prefixes. If app-level check is bypassed, all tenants are exposed.
entries.how_it_works: DynamoDB IAM supports LeadingKeys condition to restrict access to items starting with a specific prefix. Without this, the Lambda can read ANY item in the table regardless of partition. Tenant isolation relies solely on application code.
preconditions: Shared DynamoDB table (all tenants same table)
IAM grants read_write without LeadingKeys condition
Application is the only tenant isolation barrier
impact: Single bug in app code = all tenants exposed
entries.detection_guidance: Check IAM policy for DynamoDB: does it include a condition on dynamodb:LeadingKeys? CDK's grant_read_write_data() does NOT add this condition.
exploit_payloads: If Lambda is compromised: table.scan() returns ALL tenants' data
entries.vulnerable_code:
sessions_table.grant_read_write_data(agent_lambda)
# No LeadingKeys condition — Lambda can access all partitions
remediation: Add dynamodb:LeadingKeys condition to IAM policy
Use tag-based access control (ABAC) with tenant_id as principal tag
Consider separate tables per tenant for highest isolation

entries.name: Presigned URL Generated for Wrong Tenant's S3 Path
entries.description: Presigned URL generation uses tenant_id from request body, allowing user to get a signed URL for another tenant's objects.
entries.how_it_works: Lambda generates presigned URLs for S3 objects. If the S3 key (which determines which object is accessed) includes a tenant_id from the request body rather than auth context, attacker can request URLs for any tenant's files.
preconditions: Presigned URL generation accepts customer_id from body
No validation against authenticated tenant
impact: Upload files to another tenant's namespace
entries.detection_guidance: Find generate_presigned_url calls. Trace the Key parameter backward to determine tenant_id origin.
entries.vulnerable_code:
customer_id = body.get('customer_id')
key = f'{customer_id}/evidence/{filename}'
url = s3.generate_presigned_url('get_object', Params={'Bucket': B, 'Key': key})
entries.safe_code:
tenant_id = event['requestContext']['authorizer']['tenant_id']
key = f'{tenant_id}/evidence/{filename}'
# Verify the key we're signing belongs to this tenant
assert key.startswith(f'{tenant_id}/')
url = s3.generate_presigned_url('get_object', Params={'Bucket': B, 'Key': key})
remediation: Tenant_id from auth context only
Validate final key starts with authenticated tenant prefix
Log all presigned URL generations for audit

entries.name: Tenant Context from Mutable JWT Claim
entries.description: Cognito custom:tenant_id attribute is mutable — user can change their tenant affiliation via UpdateUserAttributes API.
entries.how_it_works: If custom:tenant_id is mutable=True in Cognito, users can call the UpdateUserAttributes API with their access token to change their tenant_id. Next token issued contains the new tenant_id — they now belong to a different tenant.
preconditions: Cognito custom:tenant_id is mutable
Authorizer trusts JWT custom:tenant_id claim
User has access to Cognito SDK (always true — it's a public API)
affected_components: All downstream services trusting tenant_id from JWT
entries.detection_guidance: Check CDK: is custom:tenant_id mutable=True? If so, this is exploitable regardless of app-level protections.
remediation: Set mutable=False on tenant_id and role attributes
If migration is needed: use pre-token-generation trigger to inject authoritative tenant_id from DynamoDB
Add secondary validation: authorizer checks user_tenants table to confirm membership

entries.name: Scope Bypass in RBAC (Compliance Manager Accesses All Controls)
entries.description: Scoped user (compliance_manager) accesses controls outside their assigned scope because scope check is missing or bypassable.
entries.how_it_works: The RBAC system defines scoped actions where compliance_managers can only write/evaluate controls assigned to them (resource_owner == user_id). If the tool extracts control_id from the user message (via LLM) without checking it against allowed_resource_ids, the user can specify any control.
preconditions: User has a scoped role (compliance_manager)
Tool accepts resource_id from user input or LLM extraction
Scope validation doesn't check resource_id against allowed list
impact: Modify controls assigned to other managers
Run evaluations on controls outside their scope
entries.detection_guidance: Check: when check_permission succeeds for a scoped action, is the resource_id validated against the user's allowed_resource_ids? Or can any resource_id be used?
entries.vulnerable_code:
allowed, reason = check_permission(role, 'evaluation.start_eval', user_id)
if allowed:
    # BUG: control_id from user input, not checked against scope
    control_id = body.get('control_id')
    evaluate(control_id)
remediation: After permission check passes, validate resource_id against user's scope
Scope rules stored in DynamoDB policies table
Default deny if resource not in allowed list

entries.name: Vertical Privilege Escalation via Cognito Role Manipulation
entries.description: User elevates from viewer to admin by modifying their Cognito custom:role attribute.
entries.how_it_works: Same mechanism as MT-005 but targeting the role attribute. User calls UpdateUserAttributes to set custom:role=admin, then their next JWT contains admin role.
preconditions: custom:role is mutable=True
Authorizer trusts JWT custom:role claim
RBAC uses role from JWT for permission decisions
impact: Full read/write access to all tenant resources
Can access audit logs, evidence, evaluations
entries.detection_guidance: Check: is custom:role mutable? If yes → CRITICAL regardless of other controls.
entries.vulnerable_code:
# Cognito
custom_attributes={'role': cognito.StringAttribute(mutable=True)}
# Authorizer
role = claims['custom:role']  # Trusted directly from JWT
entries.safe_code:
# Option 1: Immutable attribute
custom_attributes={'role': cognito.StringAttribute(mutable=False)}

# Option 2: Use Cognito Groups instead of custom attributes
user_pool.add_group('admins', description='Admin role')
# Groups cannot be self-modified by users

# Option 3: Authorizer verifies against policy store
role_from_jwt = claims.get('custom:role')
role_from_db = policies_table.get_item(Key={'pk': f'USER#{user_id}'})['Item']['role']
assert role_from_jwt == role_from_db
remediation: Set mutable=False on custom:role
Use Cognito Groups for role management (not self-modifiable)
Add pre-token-generation trigger that injects role from authoritative source
Verify role against policy store in authorizer

entries.name: Session ID Enumeration / Cross-Tenant Session Access
entries.description: Session IDs are predictable or user-supplied, allowing access to other users' sessions.
entries.how_it_works: If session_id is provided by the client (from request body) and used as DynamoDB sort key without verifying the session belongs to the authenticated tenant, a user who guesses another session_id can access it.
preconditions: Session_id accepted from request body
Session lookup doesn't verify tenant_id matches
Session IDs are predictable (sequential, timestamp-based)
impact: Access evaluation results from other sessions
entries.detection_guidance: Check: when loading a session by session_id, is the pk (tenant prefix) enforced? Or can any session_id be loaded regardless of tenant?
entries.vulnerable_code:
session_id = body.get('session_id')
# Loads session without tenant check
session = table.get_item(Key={'pk': f'SESSION#{customer_id_from_body}', 'sk': f'ID#{session_id}'})
entries.safe_code:
session_id = body.get('session_id')
tenant_id = event['requestContext']['authorizer']['tenant_id']
# Tenant enforced in key
session = table.get_item(Key={'pk': f'SESSION#{tenant_id}', 'sk': f'ID#{session_id}'})
# Even with correct session_id, wrong tenant returns nothing
remediation: Always include authenticated tenant_id in the session key
Generate session IDs with UUIDv4 (unpredictable)
Verify session ownership before returning data

entries.name: API Error Messages Leaking Cross-Tenant Information
entries.description: Error responses include information about resources from other tenants (existence, structure, or content).
entries.how_it_works: When a user tries to access a resource that exists but belongs to another tenant, the error message differs from when the resource doesn't exist at all. This reveals whether the resource exists in another tenant.
preconditions: Different error messages for 'not found' vs 'forbidden'
Attacker can probe resource IDs
impact: Enumerate which resources exist in other tenants
entries.detection_guidance: Check: does the handler return 404 (not found) vs 403 (forbidden) for cross-tenant access? Should always return 404 (don't reveal existence).
exploit_payloads: Probe control IDs: if 403 → exists in another tenant, if 404 → doesn't exist
entries.vulnerable_code:
if item['tenant_id'] != auth_tenant_id:
    return {'statusCode': 403, 'body': 'Access denied to this resource'}  # Reveals it exists!
remediation: Always query with the authenticated tenant's pk — resource from other tenants simply won't be found
Never return 403 for cross-tenant access — use 404
Avoid error messages that mention 'another tenant' or 'access denied'

entries.name: Cross-Tenant Data in Shared CloudWatch Logs
entries.description: All tenants' requests logged to the same CloudWatch log group. Anyone with log access sees multi-tenant data.
entries.how_it_works: Lambda functions process requests from all tenants and log to a single log group. If user data (messages, evidence content, PII) is logged, it's co-mingled. Anyone with logs:FilterLogEvents permission reads all tenants' data.
preconditions: Shared Lambda processes multi-tenant requests
User data logged (messages, names, evidence)
Log group accessible to multiple IAM principals
impact: Compliance violation (each tenant's data should be isolated)
entries.detection_guidance: Check: (1) Is user content logged? (2) Is the log group shared across tenants? (3) Who has read access to logs?
entries.vulnerable_code:
logger.info(f'Tenant {tenant_id}: evaluation result: {json.dumps(evaluation)}')
# All tenants' evaluations in same log stream
entries.safe_code:
# Option 1: Log only metadata
logger.info(f'tenant={tenant_id} action=evaluation control_id={control_id} result=pass')

# Option 2: Separate log groups per tenant (complex but strongest)
# Option 3: Use structured logging with field-level access control
remediation: Log metadata only (IDs, actions, status), never content
If content logging needed: separate log groups per tenant
Restrict log read access (principle of least privilege on logs:*)
Set log retention appropriate for compliance (not 7 days)
